coredata.dc:title: Europium functionalized silicon quantum dots nanomaterials for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Bacillus anthrax biomarker
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Zhan
Gu, Jiapei
Chen, Yuze
Zhang, Xiaoxia
Wu, Haixia
Qiao, Xiaoguang
coredata.dc:description: Bacillus anthracis spore causes anthrax to seriously threaten human health and even cause death. 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) is a unique biomarker because it is a major component of Bacillus anthracis spore. Herein, we design europium functionalized silicon quantum dots as a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe to detect DPA with high sensitivity and selectivity. With the addition of DPA, the red emission peaks were observed at 618 nm. The novel probe enables ratiometric and sensitive DPA detection over nanomolar concentrations (as low as 1.02 μM). This work provided an efficient background-free and self-calibrating method for the recognition of DPA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide
Silicon quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescent
DPA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM of SiQDs-NH2. Inset: The particle size distribution of SiQDs-NH2 by analyzing the TEM image.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of SiQDs-NH2 in aqueous solutions.
The standard curve of SiQDs-NH2 at different excitation wavelength.
The standard curve of SiQDs-NH2 at different pH.
FT-IR spectra of SiQDs-NH2 (A), SiQDs-NH2-EDTA (B), and SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu (C).
Zeta potential of SiQDs-NH2 (A), SiQDs-NH2-EDTA (B), and SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu (C).
The emission spectra of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu excited at 280 nm (blue line) and 420 nm (green line) wavelength, respectively.
Emission spectra of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu upon addition of 0–34 μM of DPA in water (⁎: there are multiple scattering artifacts around 560 nm).
Ratiometric calibration plot (fluorescence intensity at 618 nm/fluorescence intensity at 442 nm) of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu sensor upon the DPA concentration (0–34 μM).

Selectivity test of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu for DPA (34 μM) detection over 0.2 mM different interferences, respectively. (Inset: the photos of the ratiometric nanoprobe SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu under the exposure to different interferences under 365 nm UV light).
Schematic illustration for fabrication and sensing process for DPA of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu sensor.

head.ce:title: Europium functionalized silicon quantum dots nanomaterials for ratiometric fluorescence detection of Bacillus anthrax biomarker

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Key Laboratory of Function-Oriented Porous Materials, Luoyang Normal University, Luoyang 471934, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Henan Key Laboratory of Function-Oriented Porous Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacillus anthracis spore causes anthrax to seriously threaten human health and even cause death. 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) is a unique biomarker because it is a major component of Bacillus anthracis spore. Herein, we design europium functionalized silicon quantum dots as a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe to detect DPA with high sensitivity and selectivity. With the addition of DPA, the red emission peaks were observed at 618 nm. The novel probe enables ratiometric and sensitive DPA detection over nanomolar concentrations (as low as 1.02 μM). This work provided an efficient background-free and self-calibrating method for the recognition of DPA.

A new ratiometric fluorescent probe based on europium complexes functionalized silicon quantum dots is synthesized and applicated in the detection of DPA with highly selective and sensitive.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Anthrax is an infectious disease caused by the Bacillus anthracis, which seriously threatens human health. In addition, as a potential biological warfare agent Bacillus anthracis spore have attracted wide attention throughout the world. 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA) is a unique biomarker and major component in protective layers of bacterial spore [1–4]. Therefore, developing a fast and efficient DPA detection method is very important for preventing and controlling anthrax disease and biological attacks. In contrast with traditional biological detection methods which need lengthy period and complex operation, [5,6] fluorescence-based sensing methods have especially attractive owing to their highlight advantages such as simple operation, low cost, high selectivity and sensitivity, rapid response time and so on [7–21].

The lanthanides are commonly referred to as “rare earth element”, and the ions (Ln3+) have the most stable tri-oxidation state [22–24]. This preference is mainly owing to the shielding of 4f orbitals by the 5s and 5p orbitals, bringing about the unique and excellent photophysical properties of the lanthanide ions such as narrow emission bands, long fluorescence lifetime and large stokes shift [25–28]. Recently, the lanthanide ions-based luminescence detection of DPA has attracted a few special attention [29–32]. However, most of these lanthanide-based fluorescent probes detect DPA mainly focus on optical signal changes only in single wavelength fluorescent intensity. These single intensity-based fluorescent sensors cannot eliminate the influence of probe concentration, background signal fluorescence, excitation light source and excitation intensity [33,34]. Fortunately, ratiometric fluorescence method is an efficient approach, which can improve the sensitivity and eliminate fluorescence intensity fluctuations from instrumental or environmental factors [35–38]. Ratiometric fluorescent probes are based on determining the relative changes of fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths under same excitation wavelength. They also possess the advantage of improving sensitivity and accuracy [39,40].

For the past few years, quantum dots (QDs) exhibit improved photophysical characteristics as a new generation luminescent nanoprobes, which can be adjusted by controlling composition, morphology, surface structure of the material. Compared with traditional molecular fluorescence, QDs have advantages of high fluorescence quantum yield, high stability against photobleaching and long fluorescence lifetime [41–44]. Moreover, silicon quantum dots (SiQDs) have aroused considerable research interest in virtue of highly fluorescent, low cost, size-dependent multicolor fluorescence and great stability ultrahigh photostability in contrast with other QDs [45–47]. In particular, the SiQDs are potentially ideal fluorescent probes in a multitude of exciting and important fields due to their unique dominances including compatibility with further modification, size-dependent multicolor fluorescence by the quantum confinement effect, versatile and tunable surface functionalities, favorable biological compatibility and low toxicity [48–50]. However, to the best of our knowledge, utilizing europium functionalized SiQDs based ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of DPA has not been achieved so far.

Herein, we proposed an effective ratiometric fluorescence probe based on europium functionalized SiQDs for the detection of Bacillus anthracis biomarker (Scheme 1 ). Firstly, SiQDs with surface amine groups (SiQDs-NH2) were synthesized through a one-step method using (3-aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS) and ascorbic acid (AA), and the obtained SiQDs exhibited an intense green or blue fluorescence at different excitation wavelengths. Secondly, the fluorescent probe (SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu) was assembled by modifying the surface of SiQDs with ethylenediaminetetraacetic anhydride (EDTAD) and europium ions (Eu3+). SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu displayed a strong blue emission in water by the excitation of UV light (280 nm), while the red emission was easy to be observed with the addition of DPA due to the coordination of DPA and Eu3+. In consequence, this is a background-free and self-calibrating method to qualitatively and quantitatively detect DPA in water with satisfactory results.

Europium (III) chloride hexahydrate (EuCl3·6H2O), 2,6-Pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA, 99%), ethylenediamine tetraacetic Dianhydride (EDTAD), 3-(aminopropyl) trimethoxysilane (APTMS, 97%) and ascorbic acid (AA, 99.7%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd.. For comparison, control experiments were performed by replacing the DPA with various interferences such as AgNO3, Al(NO3)3, Co(NO3)2, CuCl2, FeCl3, MgSO4, MnCl2, Na2SO3, NaHS, Ni(NO3)2, ZnCl2, glutamine (Glu), glycine (Gly), D-phenylalanine (Phe), D-aspartic (Asp), m-Phthalic acid (m-PA), o-Phthalic acid (o-PA), benzoic acid (BA), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and nicotinic acids (NA) under the same conditions. All used metal salts were purchased from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory and used without further purification. Ultrapure water obtained from a Milli-Q ultrapure (18.2 MΩ cm−1) system was used in all experiments.

UV–Vis spectra were recorded on TECHCOMP spectrophotometer in the range of 250–500 nm. Fluorescence and excitation spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 150 W xenon lamp as a light source. Absolute quantum yields were measured by an integrating sphere method (Edinburgh FLS980). Infrared spectra were recorded by a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR instrument with the KBr pellet technique. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained by using a JEOL JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope. The zeta potentials were measured by a Malvern laser particle size analyzer (ZS-9000).

1 mL of APTMS was dissolved in 4 mL of deionized water in a 50 mL beaker under constant stirring and the temperature was maintained to be at 40 °C. Subsequently, 1.25 mL of AA (0.1 M) was added to the above mixture by stirring for 30 min and then cooled to room temperature naturally. The resulting solution was centrifuged to remove the small amount of precipitation, then dialyzed by ultrapure water for three days.

The above SiQDs-NH2 (50 mg) was dispersed by sonication in bicarbonate buffer (10 mL, 10 mM, pH = 9.6). 50 mg of EDTAD was added to the above mixture. After stirring for 8 h at room temperature, the mixed product was centrifuged with a high speed refrigerated centrifuge (TGL-20bR) for 10 min. And then washed with bicarbonate buffer and deionized water for four times to obtain the SiQDs-NH2-EDTA. As reported in somewhere else [51,52], ninhydrin-colorimetry method was applied for the quantitative determination of amino on the surface of probes before and after EDTA grafting. The EDTA/amino conjugation efficiency was calculated to be 72.5%.

100 μL of the above-mentioned SiQDs-NH2-EDTA (0.1 mg/mL) was added to 400 μL of deionized water. 0.5 μL of EuCl3·6H2O aqueous solution (0.2 M) was added with stirring by a Heat-Up Magnetic Agitator at room temperature for 2 h. the solution was dialyzed against water for 3 days to remove residual europium ions.

1 mL of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu water solution (0.1 mg/mL) was injected to a spectrophotometer quartz cuvette, then a series of various concentrations of DPA solution were added. The photoluminescence spectra were measured by a fluorescence spectrophotometer excited at UV light (280 nm). According to the changes of fluorescence intensity, the effect of DPA on the system can be clearly observed, and the purpose of detecting DPA can be achieved.

Amino functionalized SiQDs are prepared simply by mixing and stirring the reagents APTMS as silicon source and AA as antioxidant at low temperature in the previous reports [53–55]. As shown in Fig. 1 , the microstructure of SiQDs-NH2 exhibits highly monodispersity and the spherical particles with an average size of 2.04 nm via analysis of at least 100 samples in TEM images. The Zav of nanoprobes in solution is 2.51 nm with PDI = 0.14 (Fig. S1). According to the principle of DLS analysis, the achieved hydrodynamic size of the nanoprobe is a little larger than TEM result. While the low PDI indicates the nanoparticles were monodisperse in the solution. The UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of SiQDs-NH2 presents a broad absorption range from 250 to 400 nm, which is belong to the trypical π-π* transition band that may be attributed to the complicated band structure of SiQDs-NH2 and their random energy levels (Fig. 2 ). To examine the fluorescent property of the synthesized SiQDs-NH2, the emission spectra of SiQDs-NH2 in aqueous solution are studied. As shown in Fig. 3 , the maximum emission wavelength of SiQDs-NH2 are centered around ranging from 440 to 550 nm under the various excitation wavelength (from 280 to 480 nm). When the excitation wavelength is 280 nm, SiQDs-NH2 presents strong blue emission, and the corresponding emission spectra exhibits a red shift with the increasing excitation wavelength. It may be ascribed to the changing of fluorophores structures which result in different fluorescent properties. In addition, we investigate the fluorescence stability of the SiQDs-NH2 in various pH buffer aqueous solution. Fig. 4 shows the effect of pH on the relative emission at 442 nm (λex = 280 nm), illustrating that the fluorescence intensity of SiQDs-NH2 shows ignorable change within a wide pH range from 2 to 10. The stability of SiQDs-NH2 and the multiple amino groups on its surface will provide a good basis for further modification and great potential application as the fluorescence nanoprobes.

The FT-IR spectra of SiQDs-NH2, SiQDs-NH2-EDTA, and SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu are investigated as shown in Fig. 5 . The peaks located at 3515 and 1563 cm−1 are attributed to the NH bond bending and the NH stretching vibration respectively, and 1077 cm−1 is ascribed to the vibrational stretch of SiO bonding (Fig. 5A). The board absorbance in the range of 2867–2929 cm−1 is attributed to the CH stretching vibration of saturated aliphatic hydrocarbons on the silane chain. After attracting with EDTA, a new peak at 1670 cm−1 appears because of the stretching vibration of the carboxyl group in the FT-IR curve of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA (Fig. 5B). However, the COOH stretching vibration moves to 1460 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu (Fig. 5C), indicating that the carboxyl groups are coordinated with europium ions.

To further confirm the successful synthesis of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu nanoprobe and, the zeta potentials of SiQDs-NH2, SiQDs-NH2-EDTA, and SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu are recorded. As shown in Fig. 6 , the zeta potential of free SiQDs-NH2 is −2.58 mV, and displays weak electronegativity, while the negative charge increases sharply on the surface of the SiQDs-NH2-EDTA (−8.61 mV), indicating that most of the amino groups on the surface of silicon quantum dots are modified by EDTAD. After conjugation with Eu3+ ions, the negative charge decreases sharply on the surface of SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu (−2.92 mV), which is further evidence for the coordination between EDTAD and europium ions. Moreover, the EDX analysis has been done for SiQDs-NH2 before and after modification with Eu3+. As shown in Fig. S2, the peak for Eu and Cl can be clearly observed for SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu, indicating the successful binding of rear earth ions on the SiQDs-NH2. The amount of the Eu3+ on the surface of SiQDs-NH2 is determined to be 3.05% (Table S1). Therefore, the above information demonstrated that europium complexes modified SiQDs has been successfully assemble.

Selectivity is very crucial to the fluorescent nanoprobes. Hence, we evaluate the selectivity of europium complexes functionalized silicon quantum dots for DPA by addition of various interfering substance. As showed in Fig. 10 , various interferences such as metal cations (Ag+, Al3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+), amino acid (glutamine (Glu), glycine (Gly), d-phenylalanine (Phe), d-aspartic (Asp)), m-Phthalic acid (m-PA), o-Phthalic acid (o-PA), benzoic acid (BA), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and nicotinic acids (NA) lead to the normalized relative intensity ratio change (I618nm/I442nm) which is almost negligible. In contrast, the addition of DPA induce an obvious ratio fluorescence enhancement at the same dose. The digital photo in Fig. 10 demonstrated that the emission color change (from blue to red) can be identified by the naked eyes under UV-lamp illumination. We also explored the selectivity behavior of the nanoprobe SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu in a mixture of interferences with and without DPA (Fig. S4). The emission curves showed SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu could effectively detect DPA in the mixture of coexisting ions at high concentration (0.2 mM). Therefore, SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu is possible to be used for DPA detection with high selectivity and effective identifying for Bacillus anthracis spore.

In summary, we have synthesized a novel ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe based on lanthanide emission and silicon quantum dots by the covalent modification method. The SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu displays a great performance for highly selective, sensitive detection of anthrax biomarker (DPA) in water. In the presence of 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (DPA), the SiQDs-NH2-EDTA-Eu nanoprobe exhibits obvious luminescence color change from blue to red, which was visually recognized by ultraviolet light. The obtained nanoprobe provides a new method for the convenient and rapid detection of bacterial spores in practical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Key Scientific Research Project of Higher Education of Henan Province (17A150016 and 17A150015), Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (162300410200 and 172102210098).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2018.12.036.
